text:
Review 1:
Dr. Brennan is one of the most popular professors on campus and for good reason. Her PSYC101 lectures feel like TED talks. She's an engaging speaker who weaves in research, personal anecdotes, and demonstrations. The class is large (200+ students) but she creates a surprisingly intimate atmosphere. Exams are multiple choice + short answer. She tests on both lecture content AND textbook chapters — you need to do both.

Review 2:
Her exams are straightforward if you study both lecture slides and the assigned textbook readings. She assigns roughly 2 chapters per week from the Myers & DeWall psychology textbook. The textbook has a companion website with practice quizzes that are nearly identical in format to her exam questions — use them. Attendance is not tracked in the large lecture but she announces things verbally that aren't on slides, including what to study.

Review 3:
Brennan offers extra credit opportunities throughout the semester. Participating in psychology research studies through the department's subject pool earns up to 3% extra on your final grade. She also offers a reflection paper option for extra credit. These small boosts can matter if you're on a grade boundary. She teaches social psychology, cognitive psychology, abnormal psychology, and developmental psychology all in one semester — it's a lot of material.

Review 4:
Office hours: Tuesdays 2-4pm and Thursdays 10am-12pm in the Psych building room 412. She has a graduate student TA who also holds office hours and is excellent. Brennan is warm and accessible despite the large class size. She responds to emails but is slower during exam weeks — give her 48-72 hours. She genuinely cares whether students are learning, not just passing.

Review 5:
Grade breakdown: 3 exams (roughly evenly weighted at about 25% each) plus a final research paper (25%). The paper is a literature review on a psychology topic of your choice — she provides a list of approved topics. The paper is due the last week of class and is graded on content, argumentation, and proper APA citation format. She posts a detailed rubric. Most students do fine on the paper if they start early and visit the writing center.

Review 1:
Dr. Chen is outstanding. Best physics professor at State U, and I've taken three physics courses. She has a gift for making mechanics intuitive — she uses demonstrations in every lecture and the concepts stick. Her exams are formula-sheet allowed (she provides the sheet) and focus on problem-solving, not memorization. If you can solve her practice problems you can solve her exam problems — they're the same style, just different numbers.

Review 2:
PHYS101 with Chen is genuinely fun if you like problem-solving. The homework is online (WebAssign) and you get 5 attempts per problem with hints. She doesn't grade on attempts — only the final answer matters. This is very forgiving and teaches you to be persistent. Lectures are recorded and posted same day on the course page. She uses clicker questions during class — they count for 5% of your grade and you get credit for participation even if wrong.

Review 3:
Her exams are 50 minutes, 4-5 problems, formula sheet provided. She writes every exam herself and the difficulty is consistent — the hardest problem is always the last one and is worth more points. The midterm average is usually around 72%. She doesn't curve but offers bonus questions on every exam worth up to 5 points. Lab section is separate and graded by the lab TAs, not her — labs are weekly and you lose points if you're late.

Review 4:
Chen holds office hours Monday and Wednesday 11am-1pm in the Physics building room 104. She also holds a pre-exam review session the Sunday before each exam from 2-4pm in the main lecture hall. Attendance at review sessions is not required but the sessions are extremely useful — she basically tells you which topics to focus on. She covers mechanics, thermodynamics, and waves in PHYS101. PHYS102 with her covers electricity and magnetism.

Review 5:
Grade breakdown: WebAssign homework 15%, clicker participation 5%, 2 midterms 20% each, final 30%, lab 10%. She does not drop any grades. Students who do all the homework and attend lectures consistently almost always pass. The course is not easy but it's very learnable. She responds to emails within 48 hours. She's particularly good at helping students who are struggling — don't wait until the week before the exam to ask for help.

Review 1:
Dr. Johnson is one of the best professors I've had at State U. Her lectures are incredibly well-organized and she always connects abstract concepts to real-world examples. CS101 with her is tough but fair. The midterm is entirely based on lecture slides — she barely touches the textbook during class. Exams are multiple choice + 2 coding problems. She curves the midterm but NOT the final, so don't slack off. Office hours are very helpful; she spends real time working through problems with you rather than just pointing at the textbook.

Review 2:
Took CS101 with Johnson spring semester. Attendance matters a lot — she does in-class exercises worth 15% of your grade and doesn't post solutions afterward. Her style is very hands-on: expect live coding during lectures. She uses Python for all examples even though the course is language-agnostic in theory. The assignments are challenging but she gives partial credit generously. Avoid taking her if you hate group projects — there's one group project worth 20% that some people loved and others hated.

Review 3:
Johnson's CS101 is legitimately one of the harder intro CS courses in the department, but she prepares you well for CS201. Her exams are hard compared to other professors teaching the same course. Grade breakdown: homework 25%, midterm 25%, group project 20%, final 30%. She does NOT drop any homework grades. If you're serious about CS, take her. If you just need the requirement, take Professor Lee instead — much easier.

Review 4:
Really enjoyed her class. She's enthusiastic and clearly loves teaching. The only complaint I have is that she moves fast — if you miss a lecture you will feel lost. Her slides are posted on Canvas but they're not as useful without her explanation. She recommends coming to class even when you're tired because she gives hints about exam topics verbally during lecture. The final covers everything from the whole semester with emphasis on the last 4 weeks.

Review 5:
Dr. Johnson gave me my first real understanding of algorithms. She explains recursion better than anyone else I've encountered. Her office hours are Tuesdays and Thursdays 2-4pm in room 312 of the CS building. She's also usually available by appointment. Very responsive on email — usually replies within 24 hours. She's strict about the syllabus deadline policy: late work loses 10% per day, no exceptions unless you contact her before the deadline.

Review 1:
Dr. Lee is the easiest CS101 professor by far. If you just need to fulfill the requirement and aren't planning to major in CS, take her. Exams are straightforward — she gives a study guide beforehand and sticks to it exactly. She teaches from the textbook closely, so if you read before class you basically know everything she'll cover. Attendance is not tracked. She posts all her slides before class.

Review 2:
Lee is a good professor if you want to learn the basics without too much stress. She's very kind and patient in office hours — never makes you feel dumb for asking basic questions. Her explanations are clear but she doesn't go very deep. The CS majors in my class were bored but everyone else appreciated the pace. Homework is easy and graded on completion, not correctness.

Review 3:
I took Lee's CS101 and got an A without much effort, but then I struggled in CS201 because I hadn't really challenged myself. In hindsight I wish I'd taken Johnson or one of the harder sections. If you're pre-med or need it for another major, Lee is the right choice. If you're thinking about CS as a career, push yourself and take a harder section.

Review 4:
Her office hours are very lightly attended which means you get a lot of personal attention if you do go. She's in room 208 on Mondays and Wednesdays 1-3pm. She uses Python and the course textbook is "How to Think Like a Computer Scientist" (free online). She drops your two lowest homework grades and offers one extra credit project at the end of the semester worth up to 5 points on your final grade.

Review 5:
Grade breakdown: homework 30% (graded completion), two midterms 20% each, final 30%. She drops the lower of the two midterm grades, so you really only need to do well on one midterm and the final. Very forgiving grading policy overall. She gives feedback on assignments within a week. Would recommend for anyone who is anxious about coding or new to programming.

Review 1:
Martinez is brilliant but lectures very fast. CS201 is already a hard course and his teaching style doesn't make it easier. He expects you to have CS101 rock solid before walking in. The course covers linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, graphs, and hash tables. His exams are written (no computer) and focus heavily on time complexity analysis — know your Big-O cold. He does not curve. Ever.

Review 2:
Took Martinez for CS201 and honestly it changed how I think about programming. He's demanding but incredibly fair. Every homework problem is taken directly from past exams, so if you do the homework you understand the exams. He posts all past exams on the course website going back 5 years — use them. The final is cumulative and 40% of your grade. Three homework sets, one midterm, one final. No group projects.

Review 3:
Martinez has a reputation for being hard but I found him very approachable once I went to office hours. He holds office hours Monday, Wednesday, Friday 10am-12pm in CS building room 215. He also has a TA session Thursday evenings 6-8pm in the lab. The TAs for CS201 are usually very good — check who is TAing before you register. The worst part of the course is the AVL tree implementation project — start it at least 2 weeks early, not one. Everyone who starts it the week before regrets it.

Review 4:
His slides are dense and he goes through them quickly. Recording lectures is technically allowed but the audio quality in his room is bad. He recommends the textbook (Sedgewick) and actually assigns readings from it, unlike many professors who say "optional reading" and mean it. There are weekly reading quizzes on Canvas that are open-book but timed — 10 minutes for 5 questions. They're easy if you did the reading and impossible if you didn't.

Review 5:
Grade breakdown: reading quizzes 10%, homework 25%, midterm 30%, final 35%. He does not drop any grades. The curve for the class historically has been about 5 points added to the final grade, but he doesn't announce this until after the final — don't count on it. Students who attend every class average about a full letter grade higher than students who don't, based on what upperclassmen told me. His passion for the subject is infectious even when the material is hard.

Review 1:
Dr. Nguyen is a captivating lecturer. HIST110 covers an enormous sweep of history and she keeps it coherent by focusing on themes (trade, religion, political organization, technology) rather than just dates and names. She's explicit about what she thinks matters and why. Her exams ask you to argue a thesis, not recite facts — if you haven't internalized how to construct a historical argument, you'll struggle.

Review 2:
Her two in-class exams are essay-only: she gives you 3 prompts and you choose 2. You have 50 minutes per essay. She grades on argument quality, use of specific historical evidence, and clear writing. The essays are not graded on length — a focused 4-paragraph essay can score higher than a rambling 8-paragraph one. She provides essay prompts one week before the exam — not the exact questions, but the themes.

Review 3:
Nguyen's reading load is substantial. She assigns 3-4 articles per week plus chapters from the main textbook (Bentley & Ziegler). The readings are dense academic writing. She posts reading guides that tell you what to focus on — use them. Her lectures cover different material than the readings, so you need both. Students who only read or only attend lectures do not do as well as students who do both.

Review 4:
She's rigorous but approachable in office hours. She's in Humanities building room 215 on Tuesdays 1-3pm and Thursdays 11am-1pm. She takes academic integrity very seriously — she has a zero-tolerance policy and reports all suspected violations to the Dean of Students office. Her research paper prompt is given at week 6 and the paper is due week 13, giving you 7 weeks. She requires a thesis proposal and an annotated bibliography as intermediate checkpoints.

Review 5:
Grade breakdown: reading responses 20% (short weekly paragraphs), in-class essay exam 1 25%, in-class essay exam 2 25%, research paper 30%. No curve applied. Her grading rubric emphasizes historical thinking, not just factual accuracy. She defines historical thinking as: using evidence to make an argument, acknowledging counterevidence, and situating events in broader context. Students who understand that framework do well. Highly recommended for anyone who enjoys intellectual challenge.

Review 1:
Dr. Okafor is an incredibly engaged writing instructor. Her feedback on papers is detailed and genuinely useful — she doesn't just mark grammar errors, she engages with your ideas. ENG110 with her involves a lot of revision: you write a draft, get feedback, revise, and resubmit. She believes writing is a process, not a product. This is more work than other sections but you actually improve as a writer.

Review 2:
Okafor assigns 4 major essays over the semester plus weekly 1-page reading responses. The reading responses are pass/fail and she's lenient — just show you did the reading. The major essays are graded seriously. She uses a detailed rubric which she shares before each assignment. Plagiarism policy: she uses Turnitin and takes it extremely seriously. First violation is automatic failure of the course.

Review 3:
She's tough on paper grades — getting an A from her means your writing is genuinely excellent. The median grade in her section is usually a B. She teaches argument and research writing. By the end of the semester you'll know how to build an academic argument, find and cite sources properly, and write clearly. Very useful skills if you have any writing in your future. Office hours Tuesdays 10am-12pm in Humanities building room 301.

Review 4:
Okafor runs her class as a workshop — you read and critique each other's drafts during class. Some students find this uncomfortable at first but it gets better. She's very clear about workshop etiquette and models how to give useful feedback. Class size is capped at 20 which makes this possible. Participation in workshop counts for 15% of your grade.

Review 5:
Grade breakdown: reading responses 10%, workshop participation 15%, essay 1 (narrative) 15%, essay 2 (analysis) 20%, essay 3 (research) 25%, revision portfolio 15%. She gives you a chance to revise each essay once after getting her feedback. The revision grade replaces the original if it's higher. This policy makes the course much more forgiving if you have a rough start.

Review 1:
Park makes ECON101 interesting. He uses current events and real examples in every lecture — supply and demand isn't abstract when he's applying it to stuff you read about in the news. He's funny and keeps class engaging even in an 8am slot. His exams are multiple choice only, which is good or bad depending on whether you're a good test-taker. No partial credit — you either know it or you don't.

Review 2:
The ECON101 final with Park is comprehensive and very fair. He posts a study guide covering 20 key concepts and all exam questions come from that list. If you know those 20 concepts you will do well. He grades on a curve — the top score in the class gets an A and everyone else is scaled from there. Usually means 75-80% is a B+. His lectures are posted as audio recordings but not video.

Review 3:
Park holds review sessions before each exam in the large lecture hall — usually 6-8pm the Thursday before the exam. These are extremely popular and very useful. He goes through practice questions and tells you what to focus on. Attendance is not required but I'd estimate 80% of the class shows up. His office hours are sparse: only Fridays 1-3pm. If you need help outside of that, email him and he usually responds.

Review 4:
Grade breakdown: homework (online, via Aplia) 20%, midterm 35%, final 45%. He drops the 2 lowest homework grades. The Aplia homework is due every Sunday night and covers that week's material. It's adaptive — if you get questions wrong it gives you more practice in that area. Completing the homework consistently is the best way to prepare for exams. His class has no group projects and no papers — just exams and homework.

Review 5:
Park is particularly good at explaining consumer theory and market structures. Students consistently say the hardest part of his exams is the game theory section — it comes late in the semester and he covers it quickly. Spend extra time on game theory. His TAs hold a dedicated game theory review session which is very helpful. The course follows the Mankiw textbook but he only assigns chapters 1-12 and 14-17.

Review 1:
Thompson is one of the most polarizing professors in the math department. Either you love his teaching style or you don't. He teaches everything from first principles and gets annoyed when students just want the formula to memorize. If you're the type who wants to understand the why behind mathematics, he's phenomenal. If you want recipes to follow, take someone else. His exams require you to prove things, not just compute.

Review 2:
Calc II with Thompson is hard. He covers all the standard topics — integration techniques, series, sequences, polar coordinates — but he goes deeper than the syllabus requires. His exams are 3 hours long and he gives you 4 problems. They're hard problems. The class average on his midterms is usually around 55-60%, which he then curves to roughly a B-. If you get above the average you're in decent shape.

Review 3:
Thompson is old school — no calculators allowed on any exam, ever. He believes if you can't do it by hand you don't understand it. He's not wrong, but it makes exam prep harder. He does offer a formula sheet for the final (one page, both sides, handwritten). His homework is from the textbook and he assigns a lot of it — expect 15-20 problems per week. He checks homework but grades it generously.

Review 4:
His lectures are brilliant but dense. He writes everything on the board and rarely uses slides. If you miss a class there's no good way to catch up except borrowing notes from someone who was there. He does not post lecture notes. He moves at a pace that assumes you're reviewing material between classes. Office hours are Tuesdays 3-5pm and by appointment. He's gruff in large lectures but surprisingly warm one-on-one.

Review 5:
Grade breakdown: homework 15%, two midterms 25% each, final 35%. He does not drop grades but the curve is applied to each exam individually. The best students in his class go on to do very well in upper-division math. Several people in my section switched sections after the first midterm — if you're going to do that, do it before the drop deadline (usually week 5). He's worth the challenge if you can handle it.

Review 1:
Torres is a demanding but fair chemistry professor. He expects you to come to class having done the pre-reading and his lectures build on that foundation rather than re-teaching the textbook. CHEM101 with him covers atomic structure, bonding, stoichiometry, thermodynamics, and kinetics. His exams are problem-solving heavy — you need to be able to set up and solve multi-step problems under time pressure.

Review 2:
His exams are the hardest part of the course. He gives 90-minute exams with 8-10 problems, some of which have multiple parts. He provides a periodic table and formula sheet but not a calculator — all arithmetic is designed to work out to clean numbers. If you're getting messy numbers, you made an error. This is actually a useful check. He curves every exam: he sets the highest score as 100 and scales everyone else.

Review 3:
Torres has fantastic office hours — he holds them Monday, Wednesday, Friday 2-4pm in the Chem building room 110. He also has a TA-run help room that's open every weekday 9am-5pm and Sunday afternoons. The help room is invaluable before exams. Recitation sections are mandatory and worth 10% of your grade. The recitation TA matters a lot — some are better than others.

Review 4:
The lab component is separate and graded separately. You register for both lecture and lab together. Lab reports are due one week after each lab and take about 2-3 hours to write properly. There are 10 labs total. He is very strict about lab safety — goggles must be worn at all times and he will ask you to leave if you violate safety rules. Lab grade: 10 reports at 8% each plus participation (20%).

Review 5:
Grade breakdown for lecture: online pre-lecture quizzes 10%, recitation 10%, midterm 1 25%, midterm 2 25%, final 30%. He does not drop any grades. Students who work problem sets from the textbook consistently — not just reading but actually solving problems — do well. Chemistry is a skill, not a subject you can cram. His recommendation: do 10 practice problems every night rather than 70 problems the night before the exam.
